text: The surgeon begins the robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by making three small stab wounds at the umbilicus: one at the superior rim, one at the base, and one at the inferior rim, for trocar placement.

The surgeon begins the robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by making three small stab wounds at the umbilicus: one at the superior rim, one at the base, and one at the inferior rim, for trocar placement.

The surgeon enters the abdomen bluntly under direct visualization with a laparoscope, observing the layers of the abdominal wall as they are traversed. The abdomen is then insufflated, allowing for inspection of potential pathologies or bilateral hernias.

The surgeon enters the abdomen bluntly under direct visualization with a laparoscope, observing the layers of the abdominal wall as they are traversed. The abdomen is then insufflated, allowing for inspection of the abdominal cavity for any additional pathology or bilateral hernia.

The surgeon places a trocar in the superior rim of the umbilicus, followed by positioning robotic arms one and two at the base and inferior rim. These arms are crossed at the level of the fascia to ensure optimal flexibility during the procedure.

A trocar is placed in the superior rim of the umbilicus, followed by the insertion of robotic arms one and two into the base and inferior rim. The arms are crossed at the level of the fascia, ensuring the remote centers are aligned with the fascia for optimal flexibility.

The surgeon places the camera port first, which includes an insufflation port for CO₂. Subsequently, two five-millimeter robotic ports are inserted through the lower two stab wounds at the umbilicus. These ports are positioned to cross each other, allowing the straight robotic instruments with long wrists to perform the operation effectively.

The surgeon places the camera port first, which includes an insufflation port for CO₂. This replaces the initial five millimeter port. Subsequently, five millimeter robotic ports are inserted through the lower two stab wounds at the umbilicus, which will be crossed for the operation.

The robotic instruments, despite being straight, have long wrists that enable them to cross and work independently. The five millimeter instruments are shown with long jaws, facilitating further separation of the arms during the procedure.

The robotic arms are attached to the ports, with the camera arm being connected first, followed by arm number two on the left side to the five millimeter port, and then arm number one. The arms are positioned to cross at the level of the fascia, allowing them to work independently using long-jawed instruments.

The surgeon attaches the ports to the robotic arms, starting with the camera arm, followed by arm number two on the left, and then arm number one. The arms cross at the level of the fascia. The camera is positioned in the superior rim of the umbilicus with a thirty-degree up viewing scope, providing a clear view of the instruments and the inguinal region for dissection.

The camera is positioned in the superior rim of the umbilicus using a thirty-degree up viewing scope, providing a clear view of the instruments positioned anterior to the camera and a direct view of the inguinal region for dissection.

The surgeon begins the transabdominal preperitoneal operation by making an incision in the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect. The peritoneum is then carefully stripped from the underside of the groin, exposing the underlying structures for further dissection.

The surgeon begins the transabdominal preperitoneal operation by incising the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect. The peritoneum is then stripped from the underside of the groin using articulating wristed robotic instruments, which provide adequate separation to prevent clashing. The camera is moved in coordination with the robotic arms to maintain proper orientation and minimize conflict.

The articulating wristed robotic instruments are visible, allowing for adequate separation of the arms to prevent clashing. The camera is actively moved to maintain proper orientation and minimize conflict between the three robotic arms during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon uses enhanced robotic visualization to dissect the peritoneal hernia sac from the spermatic cord. The edge of the hernia sac is clearly visible, and blunt dissection is primarily used, with sharp or cautery dissection applied as needed. A hook cautery from the five millimeter robotic set is available for hemostasis.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the peritoneal hernia sac from the spermatic cord, primarily employing blunt dissection with occasional sharp or cautery techniques. The edge of the hernia sac is clearly visible, enhanced by robotic visualization, and a hook cautery is available for hemostasis if required.
The surgeon continues dissecting the hernia sac from the spermatic cord, ensuring it is elevated sufficiently to prevent mesh curling. The cleared area provides adequate space for mesh placement.
The surgeon continues dissecting the hernia sac along the spermatic cord, ensuring it is fully mobilized to prevent mesh curling. The cleared area provides sufficient space for mesh placement.

The surgeon inserts the Parietex ProGrip mesh through the larger 8.5mm camera port. The mesh, featuring a Velcro-like backing, is positioned using needle holders with high grip strength for precise handling and secure placement over the underlying structures.

The surgeon inserts the Parietex ProGrip mesh through the larger 8.5mm camera port. The mesh, featuring a Velcro-like backing, is positioned over the surgical site. Needle holders, used as graspers, provide high grip strength for precise handling and placement of the mesh.

The surgeon uses high grip strength instruments to efficiently strip the peritoneal hernia sac from the cord structures. The final step involves closing the peritoneum with a V-lock absorbable suture, which features a loop on the back end to anchor the suture without knot tying.

The surgeon uses high grip strength needle holders to effectively grip the peritoneum while stripping the peritoneal hernia sac during the dissection of the cord structures. The mesh is visible and can be sutured in place if necessary.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a V-lock absorbable suture, efficiently running the suture with wristed robotic instruments to ensure a secure closure without gapping. The loop on the back end of the V-lock creates an anchor, eliminating the need for knot tying.

The surgeon uses wristed robotic instruments to run the suture, securely closing the peritoneum without any gapping. The robotic technique avoids the use of tacks, which can cause chronic pain and are costly.

The surgeon completes the suture line using a V-lock suture, backing it up on itself without tying a knot. The assistant enters through the second robotic port, positioned in front of the camera, and cuts the suture using robotic scissors.

The surgeon completes the suture line by backing up the V-lock suture on itself without tying a knot. The assistant uses robotic scissors to cut the suture, entering through the second robotic port, visible in front of the camera.

Robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair can be accomplished very safely and effectively with the robotic system, potentially reducing abdominal wall injury, pain, and improving recovery. For more information, contact clarkgerhart.com.
